sections.title: Phrasal verbs
items: **go on** — continue happening or doing sth
**shut up** — stop talking; stop making a noise
**speak up** — talk more loudly so sb can hear you

exercises.title: Topic vocabulary (gap fill)
exercises.instructions: Complete the sentences with words from the box.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the sentences with words from the box.

items.sentence: The story was really ...... and made the whole class laugh.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is makes people laugh, so the correct answer is "amusing".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.sentence: Please don't ...... me while I'm trying to concentrate.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is irritate, so the correct answer is "annoy".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: Her friendly ...... made everyone feel welcome.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is way of behaving, so the correct answer is "attitude".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple is wrong when the sentence links past to now (for, since, already, yet, ever).
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links a past action to the present or unfinished time.

items.sentence: You must ...... well when we visit the museum.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is act properly, so the correct answer is "behave".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "behave" is the only word or phrase that fits both grammar and meaning in: "You must ...... well when we visit the museum.".

explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "depressed" is the only word or phrase that fits both grammar and meaning in: "He felt ...... after failing the important test.".

exercises.title: Circle the correct word
exercises.instructions: Choose the correct option in each sentence.
exercises.studentInstruction: Choose the correct option in each sentence.

items.originalSentence: It was **rude / romantic** to leave without saying goodbye.
items.completedSentence: It was rude to leave without saying goodbye.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "rude" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "rude" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "romantic" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.romantic: "romantic" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("to leave without saying goodbye.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

optionContrast.romantic: "romantic" doesn't fit here → "rude" does, because it does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("to leave without saying goodbye.").

items.originalSentence: The **naughty / shy** child drew on the wall.
items.completedSentence: The naughty child drew on the wall.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "naughty" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "naughty" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "shy" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.shy: "shy" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("child drew on the wall.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

optionContrast.shy: "shy" doesn't fit here → "naughty" does, because it does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("child drew on the wall.").

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "ridiculous" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "ridiculous" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "polite" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.polite: "polite" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence (".").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

optionContrast.polite: "polite" doesn't fit here → "ridiculous" does, because it does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence (".").

items.originalSentence: My uncle gets **bad-tempered / enthusiastic** when he is hungry.
items.completedSentence: My uncle gets bad-tempered when he is hungry.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "bad-tempered" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present simple describes habits, routines and general facts. Present continuous describes actions happening now or temporary situations.

wrongOptions.enthusiastic: "enthusiastic" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("when he is hungry.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: Look for time clues — frequency words (very often, usually) point to present simple; right now and at the moment point to present continuous.

optionContrast.enthusiastic: "enthusiastic" doesn't fit here → "bad-tempered" does, because it does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("when he is hungry.").

items.sentenceBefore: The party next door was very
items.originalSentence: The party next door was very **noisy / calm**.
items.completedSentence: The party next door was very noisy .

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "noisy" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "noisy" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "calm" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.calm: "calm" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence (".").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

optionContrast.calm: "calm" doesn't fit here → "noisy" does, because it does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence (".").

items.sentenceBefore: She has a wonderful sense of
items.originalSentence: She has a wonderful sense of **humour / stress**.
items.completedSentence: She has a wonderful sense of humour .

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "humour" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "humour" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "stress" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.stress: "stress" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence (".").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

exercises.title: Phrasal verbs (complete the gaps)
exercises.instructions: Write the correct phrasal verb in each gap.
exercises.studentInstruction: Write the correct phrasal verb in each gap.

items.sentence: ......! The bus is leaving in two minutes.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is hurry, so the correct answer is "Come on".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "Come on", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "speak up" is a phrasal verb or fixed chunk that collocates with the rest of the sentence.

exercises.title: Word patterns
exercises.instructions: Write one preposition in each gap.
exercises.studentInstruction: Write one preposition in each gap.

items.sentence: He was ashamed ...... his naughty behaviour.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the full sentence with your answer inserted — if it sounds wrong, check tense, preposition, or word form.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Passive voice focuses on the action or object, not who performs it.

exercises.title: Word formation
exercises.instructions: Complete each sentence with the correct form of the word in capitals.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete each sentence with the correct form of the word in capitals.

items.sentence: The lesson was so ...... that I nearly fell asleep. (BORE)

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is adjective from BORE, so the correct answer is "boring".

wrongOptions.bore: The root BORE cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "boring".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of BORE that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: BORE → boring: Change the word class to an adjective with the right suffix.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "boring" is the word-formation answer from the root BORE in this context.

items.sentence: The young ...... made the audience laugh for an hour. (COMEDY)

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is person from COMEDY, so the correct answer is "comedian".

wrongOptions.comedy: The root COMEDY cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "comedian".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of COMEDY that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: COMEDY → comedian: Apply the correct suffix or word form from the root.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "comedian" is the word-formation answer from the root COMEDY in this context.

wrongOptions.emotion: The root EMOTION cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "emotional".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of EMOTION that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: EMOTION → emotional: Change the word class to an adjective with the right suffix.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "emotional" is the word-formation answer from the root EMOTION in this context.

wrongOptions.hate: The root HATE cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "hatred".

explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "hatred" is the word-formation answer from the root HATE in this context.

items.sentenceBefore: If someone leaves a room crying, they are probably

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The context points to "in tears" — crying.

wrongOptions.B: "at least" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "in tears" is correct.
wrongOptions.at least: "at least" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "in tears" is correct.
wrongOptions.C: "in secret" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "in tears" is correct.
wrongOptions.in secret: "in secret" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "in tears" is correct.
wrongOptions.D: "at first" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "in tears" is correct.
wrongOptions.at first: "at first" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "in tears" is correct.

items.sentence: My dad loves to tell a ...... at dinner.

explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.
